meta.ui_subtitle: International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972 — practice (not an official exam).
meta.ui_sourcenote: Source: COLREG 1972 · USCG Navigation Rules (public domain, 17 U.S.C. §105).

questions.stem: According to COLREG Annex II, what lights shall a vessel of 20 meters or more in length engaged in trawling exhibit when hauling her nets?

choices.text: One white light over one red light in a vertical line
Two white lights in a vertical line
Two red lights in a vertical line

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Annex II section 2(a)(ii) specifies that when hauling their nets, trawlers of 20 meters or more shall exhibit one white light over one red light in a vertical line.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Annex II, which of the following conditions apply to the additional signals for fishing vessels fishing in close proximity?

choices.text: The lights shall be at least 0.9 meter apart and visible all around the horizon at a distance of at least 1 mile
The lights shall be at least 1.5 meters apart and visible at a distance of at least 2 miles
The lights shall be placed at the same level as the lights prescribed in Rule 26(b)(i) and (c)(i)

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Annex II section 1 states that the lights shall be at least 0.9 meter apart, at a lower level than the lights in Rule 26(b)(i) and (c)(i), and visible all around the horizon at a distance of at least 1 mile.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 38, which of the following time-limited exemptions applies for nine years after the date of entry into force of the Regulations?

choices.text: The requirements for sound signal appliances prescribed in Annex III
The repositioning of masthead lights on vessels of less than 150 meters in length
The installation of lights with ranges prescribed in Rule 22

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 38(h) provides that the requirements for sound signal appliances prescribed in Annex III are subject to a nine-year exemption from the date of entry into force.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 38, which of the following exemptions from the current Regulations is granted on a permanent basis?

choices.text: The repositioning of lights as a result of conversion from Imperial to metric units and rounding off measurement figures
The installation of lights with ranges prescribed in Rule 22
The requirements for sound signal appliances prescribed in Annex III

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 38(c) grants a permanent exemption for the repositioning of lights resulting from conversion from Imperial to metric units and rounding off measurement figures.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about special rules and the COLREG Rules is correct?

choices.text: Special rules made by an appropriate authority for harbors, rivers or inland waterways shall conform as closely as possible to these Rules
A Government may make special rules for ships of war with respect to additional station or signal lights
Traffic separation schemes may only be adopted by individual coastal States, not by the Organization

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 1(b) and (c) provide that special rules for inland waters shall conform as closely as possible to the COLREG Rules, and that governments may make special rules for additional lights for ships of war.

questions.stem: To which waters do the COLREG Rules apply?

choices.text: The high seas and all waters connected therewith navigable by seagoing vessels
The high seas only, excluding all inland waterways
All waters within 200 nautical miles of any coastline

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 1(a) states these Rules apply to all vessels upon the high seas and in all waters connected therewith navigable by seagoing vessels.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 2, who may be exonerated from the consequences of neglecting to comply with these Rules?

choices.text: No vessel, owner, master or crew is exonerated from the consequences of any neglect to comply
The owner of a vessel is exonerated if the master was solely at fault
A vessel is exonerated if the collision occurred in restricted visibility

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 2(a) states that nothing in these Rules shall exonerate any vessel, or the owner, master or crew thereof, from the consequences of any neglect to comply with these Rules.

questions.stem: When may a departure from these Rules be necessary?

choices.text: When special circumstances, including the limitations of the vessels involved, make a departure necessary to avoid immediate danger
When the master decides that strict compliance would slow the vessel
When operating in harbor waters governed by port authority regulations

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 2(b) states that due regard shall be had to any special circumstances, including the limitations of the vessels involved, which may make a departure from these Rules necessary to avoid immediate danger.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 20, which of the following statements about lights and shapes are correct?

choices.text: Rules in this Part shall be complied with in all weathers.
The Rules concerning shapes shall be complied with by day.
No lights may be shown between sunrise and sunset under any circumstances.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 20(a) requires compliance in all weathers, and Rule 20(d) requires shapes to be shown by day; Rule 20(c) permits prescribed lights to be shown from sunrise to sunset in restricted visibility.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 21, which of the following correctly describe the arc of visibility of a sternlight?

choices.text: An unbroken light over an arc of the horizon of 135 degrees, showing 67.5 degrees from right aft on each side of the vessel.
An unbroken light over an arc of the horizon of 225 degrees, showing from right ahead to 22.5 degrees abaft the beam on either side.
An unbroken light over an arc of the horizon of 360 degrees.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 21(c) defines the sternlight as a white light showing an unbroken light over 135 degrees, fixed to show 67.5 degrees from right aft on each side of the vessel.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 21, what color is shown on the port side as part of the sidelights, and over what arc?

choices.text: A red light showing an unbroken light over an arc of 112.5 degrees from right ahead to 22.5 degrees abaft the beam on the port side.
A green light showing an unbroken light over an arc of 112.5 degrees from right ahead to 22.5 degrees abaft the beam on the port side.
A white light showing an unbroken light over an arc of 135 degrees on the port side.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 21(b) defines sidelights as a green light on the starboard side and a red light on the port side, each showing over an arc of 112.5 degrees from right ahead to 22.5 degrees abaft the beam on its respective side.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 22, what is the minimum visibility range required for a masthead light on a vessel of 50 meters or more in length?

choices.text: 6 miles.
5 miles.
3 miles.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 22(a) specifies that on vessels of 50 meters or more in length, a masthead light must be visible at a minimum range of 6 miles.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 22, which lights on a vessel of 12 meters or more but less than 50 meters in length are required to be visible at a minimum range of 2 miles?

choices.text: A sidelight and a sternlight.
A towing light and a sternlight.
A masthead light on a vessel of 18 meters in length.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 22(b) requires sidelights, sternlights, towing lights, and all-round lights to be visible at 2 miles on vessels of 12 meters or more but less than 50 meters; a masthead light on a vessel less than 20 meters need only be visible at 3 miles.

questions.stem: A power-driven vessel underway is required to exhibit which of the following? Select all that apply.

choices.text: A masthead light forward and sidelights
A sternlight
An all-round flashing yellow light

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 23(a) requires a power-driven vessel underway to exhibit a masthead light forward, sidelights, and a sternlight; the flashing yellow light applies only to air-cushion vessels in nondisplacement mode.

questions.stem: A power-driven vessel of less than 7 meters in length whose maximum speed does not exceed 7 knots may exhibit which lights in lieu of the standard lights?

choices.text: An all-round white light and, if practicable, sidelights
A masthead light forward and a sternlight only
Sidelights and a sternlight only, with no white light

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 23(d)(ii) allows a power-driven vessel of less than 7 meters in length whose maximum speed does not exceed 7 knots to exhibit an all-round white light and, if practicable, sidelights in lieu of the standard lights.

questions.stem: A sailing vessel of less than 7 meters in length cannot exhibit the prescribed sidelights and sternlight. What is she required to have ready at hand?

choices.text: An electric torch or lighted lantern showing a white light to be exhibited in sufficient time to prevent collision
A red flare to be discharged when another vessel approaches
A bell to be sounded at one-minute intervals

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 25(d)(i) requires a sailing vessel of less than 7 meters that cannot exhibit the prescribed lights to have ready at hand an electric torch or lighted lantern showing a white light to be exhibited in sufficient time to prevent collision.

questions.stem: A vessel proceeding under sail when also being propelled by machinery shall exhibit which daytime signal?

choices.text: A conical shape, apex downwards, forward where it can best be seen
A ball shape at or near the top of the mast
Two cones with apexes together in a vertical line

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 25(e) requires a vessel proceeding under sail when also being propelled by machinery to exhibit forward where it can best be seen a conical shape, apex downwards.

questions.stem: A vessel engaged in fishing other than trawling has outlying gear extending more than 150 meters horizontally from the vessel. Which additional light or shape must she exhibit?

choices.text: An all-round white light or a cone apex upwards in the direction of the gear
A second masthead light abaft the first
Two all-round red lights in a vertical line

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 26(c)(ii) requires a vessel engaged in fishing other than trawling with outlying gear extending more than 150 meters to exhibit an all-round white light or a cone apex upwards in the direction of the gear.

questions.stem: A vessel engaged in trawling and making way through the water is required to exhibit which of the following lights? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Two all-round lights in a vertical line, the upper being green and the lower white
Sidelights and a sternlight
An all-round red light at the masthead

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 26(b) requires a trawling vessel making way to exhibit the green-over-white all-round lights and, in addition, sidelights and a sternlight.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 28, what optional signals may a vessel constrained by her draft exhibit in addition to the lights prescribed for power-driven vessels?

choices.text: Three all-round red lights in a vertical line, or a cylinder.
Two all-round red lights in a vertical line, or a ball.
Three all-round white lights in a vertical line.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 28 states that a vessel constrained by her draft may exhibit three all-round red lights in a vertical line, or a cylinder, in addition to the lights prescribed for power-driven vessels in Rule 23.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 29, what lights must a vessel engaged on pilotage duty exhibit at or near the masthead?

choices.text: Two all-round lights in a vertical line, the upper being white and the lower red.
Two all-round lights in a vertical line, the upper being red and the lower white.
A single all-round white light at the masthead.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 29(a)(i) requires a vessel engaged on pilotage duty to show two all-round lights in a vertical line at or near the masthead, the upper being white and the lower red.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 29, which additional lights must a pilot vessel on pilotage duty exhibit when underway?

choices.text: Sidelights and a sternlight, in addition to the masthead all-round lights.
An all-round flashing yellow light.
A second masthead light abaft of and higher than the forward one.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 29(a)(ii) states that when underway a vessel on pilotage duty shall, in addition to the all-round masthead lights, exhibit sidelights and a sternlight.

questions.stem: A vessel aground is required to exhibit, in addition to the anchor lights, which of the following signals where they can best be seen? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Two all-round red lights in a vertical line
Three balls in a vertical line
An all-round flashing yellow light

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 30(d) requires a vessel aground to exhibit, in addition to anchor lights, two all-round red lights in a vertical line and three balls in a vertical line.

questions.stem: Which of the following vessels is NOT required to exhibit the anchor lights or shapes prescribed in Rule 30(a) and (b)?

choices.text: A vessel of less than 7 meters in length, when at anchor not in or near a narrow channel, fairway or anchorage, or where other vessels normally navigate
A vessel of less than 50 meters in length at anchor
A vessel of 100 meters or more in length at anchor

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 30(e) exempts a vessel of less than 7 meters in length when at anchor and not in or near a narrow channel, fairway or anchorage, or where other vessels normally navigate from exhibiting the prescribed anchor lights or shape.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 31, what must a seaplane or WIG craft do when it is impracticable to exhibit lights and shapes of the prescribed characteristics or positions?

choices.text: Exhibit lights and shapes as closely similar in characteristics and position as is possible.
Extinguish all lights until conditions permit full compliance.
Display only a single all-round white light until full compliance is restored.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 31 requires a seaplane or WIG craft, when full compliance is impracticable, to exhibit lights and shapes as closely similar in characteristics and position as is possible.

questions.stem: COLREG Rule 31 applies to which types of craft when it is impracticable to comply with the normal light and shape requirements?

choices.text: A seaplane and a WIG craft.
A seaplane only.
Any vessel less than 12 meters in length.

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 31 expressly covers both a seaplane and a WIG craft when it is impracticable for them to comply with the normal lights and shapes requirements.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 32, which of the following correctly describes a 'short blast' AND a 'whistle'?

choices.text: A short blast is about one second's duration; a whistle is any sound signaling appliance capable of producing the prescribed blasts
A short blast is about one second's duration; a whistle must produce only high-pitched tones
A short blast is from four to six seconds' duration; a whistle is any sound signaling appliance capable of producing the prescribed blasts

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 32(a) defines a whistle as any sound signaling appliance capable of producing the prescribed blasts, and Rule 32(b) defines a short blast as a blast of about one second's duration.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 32, what is the duration of a 'prolonged blast'?

choices.text: From four to six seconds
About one second
From two to three seconds

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 32(c) defines a prolonged blast as a blast of from four to six seconds' duration.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 33, which of the following statements about a vessel of less than 12 meters in length is correct?

choices.text: She is not obliged to carry the prescribed sound signaling appliances but shall be provided with some other means of making an efficient sound signal if she does not carry them
She is required to carry a whistle but not a bell
She is exempt from all sound signal requirements

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 33(b) provides that a vessel of less than 12 meters in length is not obliged to carry the prescribed appliances but must have some other means of making an efficient sound signal if she does not.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 33, which sound signaling appliances is a vessel of 100 meters or more in length required to carry?

choices.text: A whistle, a bell, and a gong
A whistle and a bell only
A whistle and a gong only

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 33(a) states that a vessel of 100 meters or more in length shall be provided with a whistle, a bell, and a gong.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 36, which of the following conditions apply to any light used to attract the attention of another vessel?

choices.text: It shall be such that it cannot be mistaken for any aid to navigation
It must be a fixed white light
It shall not be used in conjunction with sound signals

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 36 requires that any light to attract the attention of another vessel shall be such that it cannot be mistaken for any aid to navigation.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 36, which of the following actions is explicitly stated as something to be avoided when using lights to attract the attention of another vessel?

choices.text: The use of high intensity intermittent or revolving lights, such as strobe lights
Directing a searchlight toward the danger
Using sound signals that cannot be mistaken for authorized signals

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 36 explicitly states that the use of high intensity intermittent or revolving lights, such as strobe lights, shall be avoided.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 12, how is the windward side of a vessel defined for the purposes of this Rule?

choices.text: The side on which the mainsail is carried
The side opposite to that on which the mainsail is carried or, in the case of a square-rigged vessel, the side opposite to that on which the largest fore-and-aft sail is carried
The side from which the wind is blowing as measured by compass bearing

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 12(b) defines the windward side as the side opposite to that on which the mainsail is carried, or for a square-rigged vessel, the side opposite to that on which the largest fore-and-aft sail is carried.

questions.stem: When two sailing vessels are approaching so as to involve risk of collision, and each has the wind on a different side, which vessel shall keep out of the way?

choices.text: The vessel which has the wind on the port side shall keep out of the way of the other
The vessel which is to leeward shall keep out of the way
The larger vessel shall keep out of the way

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 12(a)(i) states that when each vessel has the wind on a different side, the vessel which has the wind on the port side shall keep out of the way of the other.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 13, from what direction must a vessel be approaching for it to be deemed an overtaking vessel?

choices.text: From a direction more than 22.5 degrees abaft the beam of the vessel being overtaken
From a direction forward of the beam of the vessel being overtaken
From a direction abeam of the vessel being overtaken

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 13(b) states a vessel is overtaking when coming up with another vessel from a direction more than 22.5 degrees abaft her beam.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 13, which of the following statements about the overtaking vessel's obligations are correct?

choices.text: Any subsequent alteration of the bearing between the two vessels relieves the overtaking vessel of her duty to keep clear
When a vessel is in any doubt as to whether she is overtaking another, she shall assume that this is the case and act accordingly
Any vessel overtaking any other shall keep out of the way of the vessel being overtaken until she is finally past and clear

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 13(a) requires the overtaking vessel to keep out of the way until finally past and clear, Rule 13(c) requires the vessel in doubt to assume she is overtaking, and Rule 13(d) states that a bearing change does not relieve the overtaking vessel of her duty.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 14, which of the following indicate that a head-on situation exists by night?

choices.text: A vessel could see the masthead lights of the other in a line or nearly in a line and/or both sidelights
A vessel can see only the sternlight of the other vessel
A vessel sees the other ahead or nearly ahead

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 14(b) states that a head-on situation exists when a vessel sees the other ahead or nearly ahead and by night could see the masthead lights in a line or nearly in a line and/or both sidelights.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 14, when two power-driven vessels meet on reciprocal or nearly reciprocal courses so as to involve risk of collision, what action shall each vessel take?

choices.text: Each shall alter her course to starboard so that each shall pass on the port side of the other
The vessel on the starboard side shall keep out of the way
Each shall reduce speed and maintain her course

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 14(a) requires each vessel to alter her course to starboard so that each shall pass on the port side of the other.

questions.stem: Two power-driven vessels are crossing so as to involve risk of collision. Which vessel is required to keep out of the way?

choices.text: The vessel which has the other on her own starboard side
The vessel which has the other on her own port side
The faster of the two vessels

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 15 states that the vessel which has the other on her own starboard side shall keep out of the way.

questions.stem: In a crossing situation involving risk of collision, what action shall the give-way vessel avoid if the circumstances of the case admit?

choices.text: Crossing ahead of the other vessel
Altering course to starboard
Reducing speed

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 15 requires the give-way vessel to keep out of the way and, if circumstances admit, avoid crossing ahead of the other vessel.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 17, when two power-driven vessels are in a crossing situation, which course alteration is restricted for the stand-on vessel if she takes action under Rule 17(a)(ii)?

choices.text: Altering course to starboard for a vessel on her port side
Altering course to port for a vessel on her own port side
Reducing speed to allow the give-way vessel to pass

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 17(c) states that a power-driven stand-on vessel in a crossing situation shall, if circumstances admit, not alter course to port for a vessel on her own port side.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 17, when may the stand-on vessel take action to avoid collision by her own maneuver alone?

choices.text: As soon as it becomes apparent that the vessel required to keep out of the way is not taking appropriate action in compliance with these Rules
Only after the give-way vessel has altered course
Immediately upon sighting the give-way vessel

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 17(a)(ii) permits the stand-on vessel to take action to avoid collision by her maneuver alone as soon as it becomes apparent that the vessel required to keep out of the way is not taking appropriate action.

questions.stem: How shall a seaplane on the water generally behave with respect to other vessels?

choices.text: She shall keep well clear of all vessels and avoid impeding their navigation
She has priority over all surface vessels and need not keep clear
She shall comply with the Rules of this Part only when underway at taxiing speed

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 18(e) states that a seaplane on the water shall in general keep well clear of all vessels and avoid impeding their navigation.

questions.stem: Which vessels must a power-driven vessel underway keep out of the way of? (Select all that apply from the options below.)

choices.text: A vessel not under command and a vessel restricted in her ability to maneuver
A sailing vessel and a vessel engaged in fishing
Any vessel that is anchored or moored

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 18(a) requires a power-driven vessel underway to keep out of the way of a vessel not under command, a vessel restricted in her ability to maneuver, a vessel engaged in fishing, and a sailing vessel.

questions.stem: What speed requirement applies to every vessel navigating in or near an area of restricted visibility?

choices.text: Every vessel shall proceed at a safe speed adapted to the prevailing circumstances and conditions of restricted visibility
Every vessel shall proceed at half her normal service speed
Every vessel shall stop and wait until visibility improves

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 19(b) requires every vessel to proceed at a safe speed adapted to the prevailing circumstances and conditions of restricted visibility.

questions.stem: Which of the following course alterations shall, so far as possible, be avoided when a vessel detects by radar alone another vessel and takes avoiding action by altering course?

choices.text: An alteration of course to port for a vessel forward of the beam, other than for a vessel being overtaken
An alteration of course towards a vessel abeam or abaft the beam
An alteration of course to starboard for a vessel forward of the beam

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 19(d) specifies that when altering course to avoid a radar-detected vessel, an alteration to port for a vessel forward of the beam (other than one being overtaken) and an alteration towards a vessel abeam or abaft the beam shall so far as possible be avoided.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 5, by what means shall a vessel maintain a proper look-out?

choices.text: By sight and hearing as well as by all available means appropriate in the prevailing circumstances and conditions
By sight alone during daylight hours
By radar only when in restricted visibility

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 5 requires every vessel to maintain a proper look-out by sight and hearing as well as by all available means appropriate in the prevailing circumstances and conditions.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of maintaining a proper look-out under COLREG Rule 5?

choices.text: To make a full appraisal of the situation and of the risk of collision
To identify vessels by name and flag state
To log all vessels within a 5-mile radius

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 5 states that the look-out shall be maintained so as to make a full appraisal of the situation and of the risk of collision.

questions.stem: According to COLREG Rule 6, which of the following factors shall be taken into account when determining a safe speed by ALL vessels?

choices.text: The state of visibility and the traffic density including concentrations of fishing vessels or any other vessels
The characteristics, efficiency and limitations of radar equipment
The maneuverability of the vessel with special reference to stopping distance and turning ability in the prevailing conditions

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 6(a) lists factors for all vessels including state of visibility and traffic density (Rule 6(a)(i)–(ii)) and maneuverability/stopping distance (Rule 6(a)(iii)); radar characteristics at Rule 6(b)(i) apply only to vessels with operational radar.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 6, a vessel proceeding at a safe speed must be able to do which of the following?

choices.text: Take proper and effective action to avoid collision and be stopped within a distance appropriate to the prevailing circumstances and conditions
Maintain a steady course regardless of visibility
Continuously monitor radar to detect vessels at long range

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 6 requires every vessel to proceed at a safe speed so that she can take proper and effective action to avoid collision and be stopped within a distance appropriate to the prevailing circumstances and conditions.

questions.stem: Under COLREG Rule 7, when shall risk of collision be deemed to exist?

choices.text: If the compass bearing of an approaching vessel does not appreciably change
Only when two vessels are on a reciprocal course
If there is any doubt as to whether risk of collision exists

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 7(a) states that if there is any doubt, risk shall be deemed to exist, and Rule 7(d)(i) specifies that risk shall be deemed to exist if the compass bearing of an approaching vessel does not appreciably change.

questions.stem: Which of the following does COLREG Rule 7 prohibit when determining risk of collision?

choices.text: Using long-range radar scanning to obtain early warning
Making assumptions on the basis of scanty information, especially scanty radar information
Conducting radar plotting or equivalent systematic observation of detected objects

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 7(c) states that assumptions shall not be made on the basis of scanty information, especially scanty radar information.

questions.stem: Which of the following is correct regarding alteration of course or speed to avoid collision?

choices.text: The alteration shall, if circumstances admit, be large enough to be readily apparent to another vessel observing visually or by radar
A succession of small alterations of course and/or speed should be avoided
An alteration of speed alone is always preferable to an alteration of course

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 8(b) requires that any alteration of course and/or speed shall be large enough to be readily apparent to another vessel, and that a succession of small alterations should be avoided.

questions.stem: What characteristics must an action taken to avoid collision have, if circumstances admit?

choices.text: It shall be positive, made in ample time and with due regard to the observance of good seamanship
It shall always consist of a reduction in speed rather than an alteration of course
It shall be communicated to the other vessel by radio before being executed

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 8(a) requires that any action taken to avoid collision shall, if circumstances admit, be positive, made in ample time and with due regard to the observance of good seamanship.

questions.stem: Where shall a vessel proceeding along a narrow channel or fairway keep?

choices.text: As near to the outer limit of the channel or fairway which lies on her starboard side as is safe and practicable
In the center of the channel to maintain maximum sea room on both sides
As near to the outer limit on her port side as is safe and practicable

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 9(a) requires a vessel proceeding along a narrow channel to keep as near to the outer limit on her starboard side as is safe and practicable.

questions.stem: Which vessels are prohibited from impeding the passage of a vessel that can safely navigate only within a narrow channel or fairway?

choices.text: A vessel of less than 20 meters in length
A sailing vessel
A power-driven vessel of 20 meters or more in length

provenance.source: COLREG — International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea, 1972
provenance.licence: Public domain — US Government work (17 USC §105). Verbatim International Regulations (1972) as published by the US Coast Guard, navcen.uscg.gov. Attribution appreciated; reuse unrestricted.

questions.explanation: COLREG Rule 9(b) states that a vessel of less than 20 meters in length or a sailing vessel shall not impede the passage of a vessel which can safely navigate only within a narrow channel or fairway.
